text: Andy Clark's book 'Natural-Born Cyborgs' argues that humans are uniquely disposed among animals to incorporate tools into their sense of self. When a skilled carpenter uses a hammer, proprioceptive signals update so that the hammer's tip feels like an extension of the hand. Neuroscientists have confirmed this: the brain's body-map, or homunculus, expands to include tools during prolonged use. Clark claims this neural plasticity is not an accidental byproduct of motor learning but the signature of a mind built from birth to dissolve the boundary between body and implement. We do not merely use tools — we absorb them.
question: What neurological evidence does Clark cite to support the idea that humans absorb tools into their sense of self?

options.text: The prefrontal cortex grows larger in tool-using populations
The brain's body-map expands to include tools during prolonged use
Dopamine levels rise when humans handle familiar tools
Mirror neurons fire identically whether a person or a tool performs an action
